text: The laparoscopic view reveals a large, gangrenous ovarian cyst on the left side, which is completely twisted due to torsion.

The surgeon prepares to enter the abdominal cavity using Palmer's point or Misra's point, stretching the abdominal wall to position the entry below the costal margin. The entire area of the torted ovarian cyst is visible.

The surgeon introduces the Veress needle, pointing it towards the stomach, to establish pneumoperitoneum. The needle is inserted carefully due to the left-sided ovarian cyst adhered to the right side. Insufflation begins after performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm proper needle placement.

The actual pressure rises to fifteen, and the surgeon removes the varus needle. The first optical trocar is introduced, pointed towards the stomach, with an orogastric tube used to deflate the stomach.

The surgeon uses a telescope to visualize the surgical field, identifying the ovarian cyst. Under direct vision, a second port is placed using the baseball diamond concept, with measurements of eighteen and twenty-four centimeters guiding the port placement.

After placing all the ports, the surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing extensive adhesions between the right abdominal wall and the omentum, which are visibly plastered together.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to detach the gangrenous ovarian cyst from the abdominal wall. Despite its appearance as a right-sided cyst, it is actually located on the left side. A puncture is made due to the cyst's large size, which cannot be managed with a grasper alone.
The surgeon uses a suction device to aspirate the contents of the ovarian cyst, which is adhered to the right abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using gentle blunt dissection to separate recent adhesions between the omentum, sigmoid colon, and ovary. The omentum appears to be sealing the gangrenous ovary, which is visible with torsion of the tube. The decision is made to proceed with oophorectomy as the patient has completed her family.

The surgeon observes the torted pedicle of the ovary adjacent to the uterus, indicating torsion. The large cyst is visible, and the surgeon prepares to use an extracorporeal knot, specifically Mishra's knot, to tie the pedicle with number one Vicryl suture. The suture is transferred to the instrument and fed four times, while the telescope provides a clear view of the procedure.

The surgeon ties Mishra's knot on the pedicle using a suture, completing multiple winds and locks to ensure secure knotting without shearing effects. The knot is tied outside the body before being applied to the pedicle.

The surgeon uses a knot pusher to tighten a secure knot over the root of the torsioned structure. The suture is then cut, and the ovary is separated using a ligature, with options to use harmonic or scissors for the dissection.

The surgeon ensures at least six millimeters of tissue is left in front of the knot to form a secure dumbbell shape, preventing the knot from slipping. A grasper provides andromedial traction to maintain distance from the bowel.
The surgeon has separated the ovary, revealing a gangrenous cyst. The next step is to remove the cyst through the colpotomy wound.

The surgeon prepares to remove the specimen through the colpotomy route, as a large endo bag is not feasible. An assistant pushes a sponge, approximately the size of a table tennis ball, through the posterior fornix to facilitate the removal.

The surgeon lifts the uterus and inserts a sponge into the posterior fornix between the two uterosacral ligaments, creating a visible bulge. Using a harmonic scalpel, the surgeon cuts the whitish-shiny vaginal fascia, located two centimeters below the arc of the uterosacral ligament, ensuring the uterosacral ligaments remain undamaged to preserve structural support.

The surgeon uses a screwing movement to position a sponge, visible alongside a claw forceps, which has been inserted through the vagina into the colpotomy wound. The bowel is adhered to the inflamed cyst, requiring blunt adhesiolysis to separate the immature adhesion.
The surgeon uses claw forceps to hold the cyst after separating adhesions. The cyst is pulled into the vagina, where two Cocker forceps are used to hold and bisect it.

The surgeon uses a speculum to expose the surgical field, with a Valsalva maneuver holding the cervix. A Cocker forceps is used to fix the thin part of the ovary, which is then bisected. The remaining fluid is drained from the cyst using suction. The cyst is maneuvered out through the colpotomy wound using a zig-zag motion.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the cyst to avoid holding the rectum and other structures. The cyst is now more than fifty percent outside the body, and the remaining portion is being extracted. The cyst, along with the ovary, is fully removed and visible outside the surgical field, ready to be sent for histopathology.

The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the abdominal cavity, filling it with three liters of saline to wash out toxic fluid. The patient is in a supine position, and the colpotomy opening is packed with gloves and a sponge. The lavage is repeated multiple times to reduce inflammation, and the pack is removed to allow fluid drainage through the colpotomy wound.

The surgeon performs copious lavage of the abdominal cavity, shaking small bowel loops, sigmoid colon, and inflamed omentum to ensure thorough cleaning. The vaginal pack is removed to allow drainage of the lavage fluid through the colpotomy wound.
The surgeon continues to wash the abdominal cavity, repeating the lavage until the fluid becomes completely clear. The uterus covers the colpotomy wound, preventing any herniation of the bowel.

The patient's head is elevated to facilitate drainage of fluid from Morrison's pouch and surrounding areas. The gallbladder, liver, left lobe of the liver, and stomach are visible. Fluid is injected throughout the abdominal cavity using a roller multi with pressure for a second wash.

The surgeon performs copious lavage using a large jar to introduce clear fluid into the abdominal cavity. The bowel is supported, and the vaginal pack is removed to facilitate drainage. Residual fluid in the anterior cul-de-sac is suctioned out.

So two three times this lavage will be done and then you can come out. So this is very good surgery where you have the less scar because all ports are five mm and the telescope is only ten mm which is in Mishra's point. Thank you very much for watching this video.
